name: Retinol
mechanism: Converted to retinoic acid in skin; binds nuclear retinoid receptors (RAR/RXR) to upregulate collagen I/III, increase cell turnover, and inhibit matrix metalloproteinases

interactions.note: Benzoyl peroxide oxidizes and degrades retinol; use at alternate times
Combining may increase irritation; separate by AM/PM if tolerated
May over-exfoliate; start with low frequencies

regulatory_notes: OTC cosmetic ingredient in US at ≤1%

name: Tretinoin
mechanism: Directly binds RAR/RXR nuclear receptors without metabolic conversion; most potent topical retinoid for collagen stimulation, comedolysis, and pigment dispersion

interactions.note: Inactivates tretinoin chemically; separate by 12+ hours
Combined in prescription creams for acne and hyperpigmentation

regulatory_notes: Prescription-only in US and most countries

name: Adapalene
mechanism: Third-generation synthetic retinoid with selective RAR-gamma binding; more stable in light and less irritating than tretinoin; potent comedolytic and anti-inflammatory

interactions.note: Epiduo combines adapalene 0.1% + BP 2.5%; stable together

name: Niacinamide
mechanism: Precursor to NAD+/NADP+ coenzymes; inhibits melanosome transfer, strengthens tight junctions, increases ceramide synthesis, reduces sebum production, and suppresses inflammatory cytokines

interactions.note: Supports barrier during retinization; ideal pairing
Myth of incompatibility debunked; works well together

contraindications: Rare: flush-prone individuals at >5% may experience temporary redness
regulatory_notes: GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe) by FDA

name: L-Ascorbic Acid
mechanism: Electron donor neutralizing free radicals; required cofactor for prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase (collagen synthesis); reduces copper at tyrosinase active site inhibiting melanin; upregulates ceramide synthesis

interactions.note: Vitamin E regenerates oxidized vitamin C; classic CE combo
Stabilizes vitamin C and doubles photoprotection
BP oxidizes vitamin C; separate routines
Can use together if skin tolerates; sensitive skin should separate AM/PM

contraindications: Very sensitive skin may not tolerate pH <3.5
regulatory_notes: Duke Antioxidant Patent covers specific formulation with vitamin E and ferulic acid

name: Salicylic Acid
mechanism: Lipophilic (oil-soluble) beta-hydroxy acid penetrates sebaceous follicles; dissolves intercellular cement (desmolytic); keratolytic; anti-inflammatory via COX inhibition; antimicrobial against C. acnes

interactions.note: May be overly drying; separate application times initially

regulatory_notes: EU: max 2% in rinse-off, 0.5% in leave-on for <3 years old

name: Glycolic Acid
mechanism: Smallest AHA (76 Da) enables deep penetration; disrupts corneocyte adhesion via calcium chelation; stimulates fibroblast collagen I/III and glycosaminoglycan synthesis at 5-10%

interactions.note: High irritation risk; alternate days or AM/PM

regulatory_notes: EU: max 10% in cosmetics with pH ≥3.5

name: Lactic Acid
mechanism: AHA with unique humectant properties; disrupts corneocyte adhesion; increases natural moisturizing factors and ceramide synthesis; stimulates collagen at higher concentrations; antimicrobial against C. acnes

interactions.note: Niacinamide supports barrier while lactic acid exfoliates

regulatory_notes: Part of skin's natural moisturizing factor (NMF)

name: Hyaluronic Acid
mechanism: Glycosaminoglycan with exceptional water-binding capacity (holds 1000x weight in water); forms hydrating film on skin; different molecular weights penetrate to different depths; stimulates CD44 receptor signaling for tissue repair
regulatory_notes: Multiple molecular weights available for different depths
Sodium hyaluronate is the salt form with better stability

name: Benzoyl Peroxide
mechanism: Releases free oxygen radicals that oxidize bacterial proteins; potent bactericidal against C. acnes; mildly keratolytic; no bacterial resistance develops

interactions.note: Oxidizes and degrades retinol; never use together

name: Azelaic Acid
mechanism: Dicarboxylic acid with dual anti-acne and anti-hyperpigmentation action; inhibits tyrosinase (competitive), reduces abnormal melanocyte activity, antibacterial against C. acnes, anti-inflammatory, normalizes keratinization

interactions.note: Both acidic; may irritate sensitive skin

name: Zinc Oxide
mechanism: Inorganic UV filter that reflects and scatters UVA/UVB rays via physical barrier; broad-spectrum protection; anti-inflammatory (zinc modulates immune response); antimicrobial; wound healing support
regulatory_notes: FDA GRASE (Generally Recognized as Safe and Effective)
Only UV filter approved for infants <6 months

name: Titanium Dioxide
mechanism: Inorganic UV filter reflecting/scattering UVB and short UVA; excellent UVB protection; less UVA coverage than zinc oxide; photocatalytic activity neutralized by coating (silica, alumina)

interactions.note: Improves visible light protection and tint

regulatory_notes: Must be coated to prevent photocatalytic ROS generation

name: Ceramides
mechanism: Major lipid component (40-50%) of stratum corneum; forms lamellar bilayers preventing TEWL; signals keratinocyte differentiation; restores barrier function in deficient skin (eczema, aging)

interactions.note: Optimal barrier repair requires 1:2:1 ceramides:cholesterol:fatty acids ratio

regulatory_notes: Multiple types (NP, AP, EOP, NG) with slightly different functions

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4 (Matrixyl)
mechanism: Signal peptide mimicking procollagen I fragment; KTTKS sequence activates fibroblasts to produce collagen types I, III, and IV; palmitoyl chain enhances skin penetration

name: Acetyl Hexapeptide-8 (Argireline)
mechanism: Neuropeptide mimicking SNAP-25; competes for SNARE complex formation reducing acetylcholine release; relaxes facial muscles reducing expression lines

name: Squalane
mechanism: Saturated hydrocarbon mimicking human sebum (squalene); lightweight occlusive preventing TEWL; non-comedogenic; antioxidant; enhances penetration of other actives
regulatory_notes: Squalane is the stable hydrogenated form of squalene

name: Centella Asiatica
mechanism: Rich in triterpenoids (asiaticoside, madecassoside, asiatic acid, madecassic acid); stimulates collagen I/III synthesis; anti-inflammatory via NF-κB inhibition; promotes wound healing and angiogenesis; strengthens capillaries
regulatory_notes: Traditional medicinal herb with modern clinical validation
Madecassoside is the most active compound

name: Snail Mucin
mechanism: Rich in glycoproteins, hyaluronic acid, glycolic acid, allantoin, collagen, elastin, and antimicrobial peptides; promotes wound healing, hydration, and skin repair; stimulates fibroblast activity

name: Propolis
mechanism: Resinous mixture with flavonoids (galangin, quercetin), phenolic acids, and terpenes; broad-spectrum antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and wound healing properties; stimulates collagen synthesis
regulatory_notes: May cause staining in high concentrations

name: Tea Tree Oil
mechanism: Terpinen-4-ol disrupts microbial cell membranes; broad-spectrum antimicrobial (C. acnes, S. aureus, Malassezia); anti-inflammatory via cytokine suppression; does not generate free radicals like benzoyl peroxide
benefits: Less irritating than BP for some

name: Hydroquinone
mechanism: Competitive inhibitor of tyrosinase; disrupts melanin synthesis by interfering with melanocyte metabolism; causes selective destruction of melanocytes at high concentrations/extended use

interactions.note: Triple therapy: HQ + tretinoin + steroid is gold standard for melasma
Both treat hyperpigmentation; may irritate combined

regulatory_notes: OTC at 2% in US (restricted access in some states)
EU: banned in cosmetics since 2001 (available by prescription only)
Potential carcinogenic concerns at very high concentrations

name: Kojic Acid
mechanism: Chelates copper at tyrosinase active site inhibiting melanin production; byproduct of rice fermentation (Aspergillus oryzae); also has antioxidant and mild antimicrobial properties
contraindications: Sensitive skin may develop contact dermatitis
regulatory_notes: US: allowed in cosmetics up to 1%
Can cause contact sensitization in some individuals

name: Alpha Arbutin
mechanism: Glycosylated hydroquinone that releases hydroquinone slowly via hydrolysis; inhibits tyrosinase without cytotoxicity to melanocytes; more stable and less irritating than beta-arbutin or hydroquinone

name: Tranexamic Acid
mechanism: Synthetic amino acid inhibiting plasminogen activator; reduces arachidonic acid and prostaglandin release; decreases UV-induced melanocyte activity and melanin synthesis; reduces blood vessel growth in melasma
contraindications: History of thrombosis or clotting disorders
Oral: do not combine with hormonal contraceptives without medical supervision

name: Ferulic Acid
mechanism: Potent phenolic antioxidant scavenging free radicals; stabilizes vitamin C and vitamin E via redox cycling; enhances photoprotection (8x vs vitamin C alone); anti-inflammatory via NF-κB inhibition
benefits: Synergistic with vitamins C and E

interactions.note: Doubles vitamin C efficacy and stability

regulatory_notes: Duke Antioxidant Patent covers specific C+E+Ferulic formulation
Naturally found in rice bran, oats, coffee, apples
Ideal pH <3.5 for vitamin C synergy

name: Vitamin E
mechanism: Lipid-soluble antioxidant protecting cell membranes from oxidative damage; donates electrons to neutralize free radicals; regenerates oxidized vitamin C (redox cycle); anti-inflammatory via NF-κB inhibition; supports skin barrier
concerns_exacerbated: acne (at very high concentrations in oily skin)

interactions.note: Classic redox cycle; each regenerates the other

contraindications: May exacerbate acne in very oily skin at >2%
regulatory_notes: Tocopheryl acetate is more stable but requires enzymatic conversion

name: Green Tea Extract
mechanism: EGCG is primary catechin (50-80% of total); inhibits UV-induced inflammation and immunosuppression; suppresses matrix metalloproteinases; anti-inflammatory via COX-2 inhibition; modulates androgen receptors reducing sebum

name: Resveratrol
mechanism: Polyphenol from grapes/berries; activates SIRT1 (longevity gene) and AMPK; potentiates Nrf2 antioxidant pathway; inhibits NF-κB and COX-2; anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and tyrosinase-inhibiting properties
regulatory_notes: Unstable in light and air; requires proper packaging
Micronized or encapsulated forms improve stability
Derived from Japanese knotweed or grapes

name: Bakuchiol
mechanism: Meroterpene from Psoralea corylifolia seeds; activates retinoid receptors and retinol-like gene expression without retinoid side effects; stimulates collagen I/III/IV, reduces MMPs, inhibits melanin via tyrosinase

interactions.note: Redundant; use one or the other

regulatory_notes: Not structurally a retinoid but functionally similar

name: Copper Peptides
mechanism: GHK (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine) tripeptide binds copper ions; promotes wound healing via TGF-β stimulation; increases collagen, elastin, and glycosaminoglycan synthesis; anti-inflammatory; antioxidant via copper/zinc superoxide dismutase

interactions.note: Vitamin C may oxidize copper peptides; separate by 30+ minutes

regulatory_notes: GHK-Cu is the most studied form
First discovered in human plasma in 1973

name: Growth Factors
mechanism: Cell signaling proteins (EGF, TGF-β, FGF, PDGF) binding to cell surface receptors; activate intracellular signaling cascades (MAPK/ERK, PI3K/AKT) stimulating proliferation, migration, and differentiation of keratinocytes and fibroblasts
contraindications: Pregnancy (theoretical concern about stimulating cell proliferation)
regulatory_notes: High cost relative to evidence level
Nobel Prize 1986 for discovery of EGF (Cohen & Levi-Montalcini)

name: Adenosine
mechanism: Nucleoside involved in cellular energy transfer (ATP/ADP); increases collagen I/III synthesis; inhibits elastase (MMP-12) preventing elastin breakdown; anti-inflammatory via adenosine receptor activation
regulatory_notes: Often combined with niacinamide in K-beauty

name: Allantoin
mechanism: Naturally occurring in comfrey; promotes cell proliferation and wound healing; keratolytic at higher concentrations; soothes irritated skin; stimulates fibroblast activity
regulatory_notes: Safe for all ages including infants
Can be synthetic or derived from comfrey

name: Bisabolol
mechanism: Sesquiterpene alcohol from chamomile/Candeia tree; inhibits COX-2 and lipoxygenase; suppresses NF-κB signaling; inhibits melanin synthesis; enhances penetration of other actives; antimicrobial
regulatory_notes: One of the safest active ingredients
Penetration enhancer property valuable in formulations

name: Colloidal Oatmeal
mechanism: FDA-recognized skin protectant; avenanthramides inhibit NF-κB and reduce inflammatory cytokines; beta-glucan and starches form hydrating film; saponins gently cleanse; lipids restore barrier; anti-itch via histamine inhibition
contraindications: Oat allergy (rare; usually protein allergy, not oat kernel lipids)
regulatory_notes: Only natural ingredient with FDA skin protectant designation

name: Mandelic Acid
mechanism: Largest AHA molecule (152 Da); slower, more controlled penetration than glycolic; antibacterial properties against C. acnes; promotes even melanin distribution; stimulates collagen with less irritation
regulatory_notes: Preferred AHA for darker skin tones due to lower PIH risk

name: Gluconolactone
mechanism: Polyhydroxy acid with larger molecular structure than AHAs; chelates metal ions providing antioxidant protection; gently dissolves intercellular bonds; natural humectant; strengthens barrier while exfoliating

name: Retinaldehyde
mechanism: Direct precursor to retinoic acid (one conversion step vs two for retinol); more potent than retinol with less irritation than tretinoin; binds RAR receptors after conversion

interactions.note: May irritate if combined; separate AM/PM

regulatory_notes: Intermediate potency between retinol and tretinoin

name: Sodium Ascorbyl Phosphate
mechanism: Stable, water-soluble vitamin C derivative; converts to ascorbic acid in skin after enzymatic cleavage; antioxidant, collagen-boosting, and tyrosinase-inhibiting properties; antimicrobial against C. acnes

name: Magnesium Ascorbyl Phosphate
mechanism: Stable, water-soluble vitamin C derivative; converts to ascorbic acid in skin; inhibits tyrosinase; stimulates collagen; less potent but more stable than L-AA
regulatory_notes: More stable than L-ascorbic acid at neutral pH
Less potent than L-AA but better tolerated

name: Ascorbyl Palmitate
mechanism: Fat-soluble vitamin C ester; penetrates lipid membranes; antioxidant in lipid phase; converts to ascorbic acid in skin; stabilizes vitamin E in cell membranes
regulatory_notes: Less research than L-AA or SAP

name: Madecassoside
mechanism: Most active triterpenoid from Centella asiatica; stimulates collagen I/III synthesis; anti-inflammatory via NF-κB and MAPK inhibition; promotes wound healing and angiogenesis; strengthens capillaries
regulatory_notes: Pure form of Centella's most active compound
Popular in K-beauty and French pharmacy

name: Ursolic Acid
mechanism: Pentacyclic triterpenoid from rosemary, apple peels, and holy basil; stimulates collagen synthesis; inhibits collagenase (MMP-1); anti-inflammatory; antimicrobial; promotes ceramide synthesis
regulatory_notes: Poor water solubility requires formulation expertise

name: Glycine Soja (Soybean) Extract
mechanism: Isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) with weak phytoestrogenic activity; stimulate collagen and elastin synthesis; inhibit MMPs; antioxidant; inhibit melanin transfer from melanocytes to keratinocytes
regulatory_notes: Genistein is the most studied isoflavone
May help with melasma in some studies

name: Licorice Root Extract
mechanism: Glabridin inhibits tyrosinase and scavenges free radicals; licochalcone A is anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial; glycyrrhetinic acid soothes inflammation; inhibits melanin production without cytotoxicity
regulatory_notes: Glabridin is the primary brightening compound
Popular in Asian and sensitive skin formulations

name: Panthenol
mechanism: Converted to pantothenic acid (vitamin B5) in skin; penetrates deeply; attracts and binds water; stimulates fibroblast proliferation; promotes wound healing; reduces TEWL; anti-inflammatory
regulatory_notes: FDA-approved for wound healing at higher concentrations

name: Cholesterol
mechanism: Essential lipid for skin barrier (25% of barrier lipids); maintains membrane fluidity; prevents TEWL; works in specific ratio with ceramides and fatty acids for optimal barrier repair; involved in cell differentiation signaling
regulatory_notes: One of three essential barrier lipids
Often sourced from wool (lanolin) or plant sterols

name: Sulfur
mechanism: Keratolytic and antibacterial; converts to pentathionic acid and hydrogen sulfide inhibiting C. acnes; antifungal against Malassezia; reduces sebum production; comedolytic; anti-inflammatory
regulatory_notes: One of oldest acne treatments (used since Roman times)

name: Beta-Glucan
mechanism: Polysaccharide activating macrophages and Langerhans cells; stimulates fibroblast collagen production; promotes wound healing; reduces inflammatory cytokines; forms protective hydrating film; antioxidant
regulatory_notes: Source-dependent efficacy: yeast-derived has strongest evidence
Also found in oats and mushrooms

name: Urea
mechanism: Natural moisturizing factor component; humectant at 5-10% attracting water to stratum corneum; keratolytic at 10-40% dissolving desmosomal connections; stimulates barrier protein synthesis; antimicrobial; penetration enhancer
contraindications: Can sting on broken skin at high concentrations

name: Caffeine
mechanism: Vasoconstrictor reducing visible blood vessels and puffiness; phosphodiesterase inhibitor with anti-inflammatory effects; antioxidant scavenging free radicals; may stimulate lipolysis (cellulite treatment)
contraindications: Not effective for pigmented or structural dark circles
regulatory_notes: Most effective for vascular dark circles (blue/purple)

name: Astaxanthin
mechanism: Carotenoid from microalgae; 6000x more potent than vitamin C; spans cell membrane bilayer providing comprehensive antioxidant protection; reduces UV-induced oxidative stress; anti-inflammatory; inhibits MMPs
regulatory_notes: Orange color can stain at high concentrations

name: Glycerin
mechanism: Small molecule humectant attracting water from environment and deeper skin layers; holds up to 25x its weight in water; stimulates ceramide production; osmolyte protecting cells; penetration enhancer; maintains moist wound healing environment
contraindications: In very dry climates, can draw water from skin if not paired with occlusive
regulatory_notes: One of most researched skincare ingredients

name: Honey
mechanism: Natural humectant with antibacterial (H2O2 production, low pH, high osmolarity), anti-inflammatory (polyphenols), and wound-healing properties; stimulates tissue regeneration and angiogenesis; maintains moist wound environment
regulatory_notes: Medical-grade honey (MediHoney) used in clinical wound care
Manuka UMF 10+ or MGO 250+ for strongest antibacterial properties
FDA-cleared medical device for wound management

name: Alpha Arbutin
mechanism: Glycosylated hydroquinone; slowly releases hydroquinone via hydrolysis; inhibits tyrosinase without destroying melanocytes; more stable and less irritating than beta-arbutin; safe for long-term use

name: Phytosphingosine
mechanism: Sphingoid base precursor to ceramides; antimicrobial against C. acnes; anti-inflammatory; regulates skin cell differentiation and apoptosis; supports barrier function

name: Ectoin
mechanism: Extremolyte from halophilic bacteria; forms protective hydration shell around biomolecules and cells; stabilizes proteins and cell membranes under stress; anti-inflammatory; UV-protective; supports barrier repair

name: Hemp Seed Oil
mechanism: Rich in linoleic acid (50-60%) and alpha-linolenic acid (15-20%); optimal 3:1 omega-6 to omega-3 ratio; anti-inflammatory; non-comedogenic (rating 0); strengthens barrier; regulates sebum

name: Rosehip Oil
mechanism: Rich in linoleic acid (44%), alpha-linolenic acid (33%), and natural tretinoin (all-trans retinoic acid, ~0.04 mg/g); promotes cell regeneration; anti-inflammatory; antioxidant (vitamin C, E, carotenoids)
concerns_exacerbated: active-acne (may be too rich for some)

name: Jojoba Oil
mechanism: Liquid wax ester structurally similar to human sebum; non-comedogenic; regulates sebum production; anti-inflammatory (myristic acid); antimicrobial; forms protective film without occlusion
regulatory_notes: Actually a wax, not an oil
Structurally similar to human sebum (25% wax esters)

name: Shea Butter
mechanism: Rich in triterpenes (lupeol, alpha-amyrin) with anti-inflammatory properties; high stearic and oleic acid content provides emollience; cinnamic acid esters offer mild UV protection; promotes collagen production
benefits: Rich in vitamins A and E
contraindications: Shea/nut allergy (rare; shea is seed, not tree nut)
Very oily or acne-prone skin may find too rich

name: Safflower Seed Oil
mechanism: High linoleic acid content (70-80%); helps dissolve sebum; anti-inflammatory; supports barrier function; linoleic acid deficiency linked to acne; non-comedogenic
regulatory_notes: High linoleic variety preferred for skin

name: Dimethicone
mechanism: Silicone polymer forming breathable occlusive film; reduces TEWL without clogging pores; smooths skin texture; enhances spreadability of other ingredients; protects from irritants
regulatory_labels: Can be washed off with cleanser

name: Zinc PCA
mechanism: Zinc complexed with pyrrolidone carboxylic acid (natural moisturizing factor); sebum-regulating; antimicrobial against C. acnes; anti-inflammatory; maintains skin hydration while controlling oil
regulatory_notes: More hydrating than zinc oxide or zinc sulfate

name: Sea Buckthorn Oil
mechanism: Richest plant source of palmitoleic acid (omega-7, 30-40%); high in vitamins C, E, beta-carotene; anti-inflammatory flavonoids; promotes tissue regeneration and wound healing; rare omega-7 supports barrier
regulatory_notes: Seed oil (lighter, more linoleic) vs berry oil (richer, more omega-7)
Berry oil is bright orange due to carotenoids

name: Probiotics / Lysate
mechanism: Lysates (non-live bacterial fragments) support skin microbiome balance; stimulate ceramide and antimicrobial peptide production; reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines; strengthen barrier; promote beneficial bacteria
regulatory_notes: Lysate (non-live) preferred over live probiotics for stability

name: Tamanu Oil
mechanism: Unique calophyllolide and xanthones with anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and wound-healing properties; promotes tissue regeneration; analgesic; rich in fatty acids (oleic, linoleic, palmitic)

name: Lactobionic Acid
mechanism: Polyhydroxy acid (PHA) with additional gluconic acid moiety; gentle exfoliation; strong antioxidant via metal chelation; humectant; barrier-strengthening; anti-aging with less irritation than AHAs

name: Cica / Madecassoside Complex
mechanism: Combination of whole Centella asiatica extract + isolated madecassoside; comprehensive triterpenoid profile; synergistic anti-inflammatory, wound healing, and collagen stimulation
regulatory_notes: Combined form offers full spectrum of Centella compounds
Popular in Korean and French pharmacy skincare

name: Heparan Sulfate Analog
mechanism: Mimics natural heparan sulfate in extracellular matrix; regulates growth factor signaling; supports barrier function; anti-inflammatory; promotes cell communication and tissue organization
regulatory_notes: Sente Dermal Repair Cream contains HSA

name: Azelaoyl Diglycine
mechanism: Water-soluble derivative of azelaic acid; more stable and better tolerated; inhibits tyrosinase; normalizes keratinization; anti-inflammatory; works at neutral pH

name: N-Acetyl Glucosamine
mechanism: Building block of hyaluronic acid and glycosaminoglycans; inhibits tyrosinase glycosylation reducing melanin production; promotes hyaluronic acid synthesis; supports barrier
contraindications: Shellfish allergy (caution - structural similarity to chitin)

name: Thioctic Acid
mechanism: Universal antioxidant soluble in water and lipids; recycles other antioxidants (vitamins C, E, glutathione); anti-inflammatory; improves cellular energy metabolism; reduces UV damage
contraindications: May be too acidic for very sensitive skin

name: Glutathione
mechanism: Master intracellular antioxidant; tripeptide (glutamyl-cysteinyl-glycine); neutralizes free radicals; recycles vitamins C and E; inhibits melanin synthesis via tyrosinase inactivation; detoxifies
regulatory_notes: Poor topical penetration; liposomal/encapsulated forms preferred

name: Pomegranate Extract
mechanism: Rich in ellagic acid and punicalagins (potent polyphenols); inhibits tyrosinase; anti-inflammatory; promotes collagen synthesis; protects against UV damage; antimicrobial
regulatory_notes: Ellagic acid is the primary skin-active compound
Seed extract has higher ellagic acid than fruit extract

name: Coenzyme Q10
mechanism: Lipid-soluble antioxidant in mitochondrial electron transport chain; neutralizes free radicals in cell membranes; energizes skin cells; anti-inflammatory; supports collagen; levels decline with age

interactions.note: CoQ10 is regenerated by vitamin E

regulatory_notes: Naturally produced in body; declines after age 30
Ubiquinol (reduced form) more bioactive than ubiquinone

name: Silymarin
mechanism: Flavonolignan complex from milk thistle; potent antioxidant scavenging free radicals; anti-inflammatory; inhibits UV-induced damage and immunosuppression; supports DNA repair
regulatory_notes: Silibinin is the most active flavonolignan
Well-studied for liver health; emerging skin benefits

name: Idebenone
mechanism: Synthetic analog of CoQ10 with smaller molecular size for better penetration; potent antioxidant; protects against UV and pollution-induced oxidative stress; energizes skin cells; anti-inflammatory
regulatory_notes: Key ingredient in Prevage by Elizabeth Arden

name: Epidermal Growth Factor (EGF)
mechanism: Recombinant 53-amino acid polypeptide mimicking human EGF; binds EGFR activating MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways; stimulates keratinocyte proliferation, fibroblast activity, and collagen synthesis
regulatory_notes: Controversial efficacy in cosmetic topical use

name: Methylsulfonylmethane
mechanism: Organic sulfur compound; supports collagen and keratin production; anti-inflammatory; promotes wound healing; enhances skin permeability for other actives

interactions.note: Sulfur supports collagen synthesis alongside vitamin C

regulatory_notes: Naturally occurring in plants and animals

name: Chamomile Extract
mechanism: Contains bisabolol, chamazulene, and flavonoids; potent anti-inflammatory (comparable to hydrocortisone in some studies); soothes irritation; promotes wound healing; anti-allergic
regulatory_notes: German chamomile (Matricaria recutita) preferred over Roman
Standardized extracts with defined bisabolol content preferred

name: Aloe Vera
mechanism: Polysaccharides (acemannan) form hydrating film; anti-inflammatory via bradykinase inhibition; wound healing through fibroblast stimulation; antimicrobial; promotes collagen synthesis
regulatory_notes: Inner leaf gel preferred over whole leaf (less aloin)
Aloin can cause irritation in some

name: Witch Hazel
mechanism: Tannins (hamamelitannin, gallic acid) act as astringents precipitating proteins; flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol) provide anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity; antimicrobial; reduces oiliness
regulatory_notes: Alcohol-free formulations preferred for sensitive skin

name: Kojic Acid Dipalmitate
mechanism: Esterified form of kojic acid with palmitic acid; more stable and oil-soluble than kojic acid; inhibits tyrosinase; converts to kojic acid in skin via esterase enzymes
regulatory_notes: More stable than kojic acid for formulation

name: 3-O-Ethyl Ascorbic Acid
mechanism: Modified vitamin C with ethyl group at C3 position; highly stable; both water and oil soluble; penetrates skin and converts to ascorbic acid; inhibits tyrosinase; stimulates collagen
regulatory_notes: One of the most stable vitamin C derivatives
Popular in Japanese and Korean skincare
Converts to L-ascorbic acid in skin

name: Tetrahexyldecyl Ascorbate
mechanism: Oil-soluble vitamin C ester; penetrates skin deeply via lipid pathways; converts to ascorbic acid intracellularly; stable in formulation; stimulates collagen; inhibits tyrosinase
regulatory_notes: Penetrates deeper than water-soluble vitamin C forms
Higher cost than other vitamin C derivatives

name: Ascorbyl Glucoside
mechanism: Glucoside-conjugated vitamin C; highly stable; converts to ascorbic acid via alpha-glucosidase in skin; inhibits tyrosinase; antioxidant; stimulates collagen

interactions.note: Safe to combine at neutral pH

regulatory_notes: Used by Shiseido and other Japanese brands

name: Marine Collagen
mechanism: Hydrolyzed collagen peptides from marine sources; film-forming hydrating properties; provides amino acids (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline); some peptides may signal fibroblast activity
regulatory_notes: Oral marine collagen has stronger evidence than topical

name: Retinyl Palmitate
mechanism: Ester form of vitamin A (retinol + palmitic acid); least potent topical retinoid; requires conversion to retinol then retinal then retinoic acid; antioxidant; mild cell turnover stimulation
regulatory_notes: Requires three enzymatic conversions to become active
More stable than retinol but much weaker
Controversial photocarcinogenic concerns in some studies (at very high concentrations with UV exposure)

name: Retinyl Retinoate
mechanism: Hybrid molecule combining retinol and retinoic acid; more stable than retinol; less irritating than tretinoin; directly provides retinoic acid component while slowly releasing retinol

interactions.note: May irritate if combined; separate AM/PM

regulatory_notes: More stable and less irritating than retinol
Patented ingredient (Time Revolution, Some By Mi)

name: Ceramide NP
mechanism: Restores lipid barrier by replenishing intercellular lipid matrix

name: Ceramide AP
mechanism: Strengthens skin barrier and prevents moisture loss

name: Ceramide EOP
mechanism: Critical for lamellar membrane formation in stratum corneum

name: Cholesterol
mechanism: Essential component of lipid bilayer; works synergistically with ceramides and fatty acids

interactions.note: Optimal barrier repair at 3:1:1 ceramide:cholesterol:fatty acid ratio

name: Avobenzone
mechanism: UVA absorber (310-400nm); degrades in sunlight without stabilizers

name: Homosalate
mechanism: UVB absorber (295-315nm)
contraindications: potential endocrine disruptor at high concentrations

name: Octisalate
mechanism: UVB absorber; stabilizes avobenzone

name: Octocrylene
mechanism: UVB/UVA absorber; stabilizes avobenzone

name: Mexoryl XL
mechanism: Broad-spectrum UVA/UVB filter; photostable
regulatory_notes: Key filter in La Roche-Posay Anthelios

name: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1
mechanism: Stimulates collagen synthesis; signal peptide for wound healing

name: Palmitoyl Tetrapeptide-7
mechanism: Reduces inflammation by suppressing IL-6; complements Pal-GHK

name: Citric Acid
mechanism: AHA exfoliant and pH adjuster; antioxidant
regulatory_notes: Commonly used as pH adjuster at low concentrations

name: Betaine Salicylate
mechanism: BHA derivative; gentler alternative to salicylic acid; oil-soluble exfoliant
regulatory_notes: Popular in K-beauty formulations as gentler BHA alternative

name: Sodium Hyaluronate
mechanism: Hyaluronic acid salt; smaller molecule penetrates deeper; holds 1000x weight in water
regulatory_notes: Low MW penetrates deeper; high MW stays on surface

name: Ubiquinone
mechanism: Endogenous antioxidant; protects mitochondria from oxidative damage; declines with age

name: Royal Jelly
mechanism: Rich in amino acids, vitamins B complex, and minerals; anti-inflammatory and collagen-boosting

name: Manuka Honey
mechanism: Antibacterial (MGO), anti-inflammatory, wound healing; humectant

name: Oryza Sativa Bran Extract
mechanism: Rich in oryzanol, ferulic acid, and vitamin E; antioxidant and brightening

name: Linoleic Acid
mechanism: Essential fatty acid; component of ceramides; anti-inflammatory; sebum quality improver
regulatory_notes: Essential fatty acid - cannot be synthesized by body
Deficiency linked to acne and dry skin

name: Oleic Acid
mechanism: Penetration enhancer; component of sebum; at high levels can disrupt barrier
regulatory_notes: Can be comedogenic at high concentrations

name: Retinyl Propionate
mechanism: Ester form of retinol; converts to retinol then retinoic acid; gentler than retinol

interactions.note: Don't double up retinoids; choose one

regulatory_notes: Environ AVST step-up system uses retinyl propionate as entry retinoid

name: Mexoryl SX
mechanism: Organic UVA filter absorbing short UVA (290-390nm); highly photostable; water-soluble; does not penetrate skin; prevents UVA-induced photoaging and pigmentation

interactions.note: Combined for comprehensive UVA/UVB protection (Anthelios formula)
Combined mineral + organic for broad spectrum

name: Mexoryl 400
mechanism: Broad-spectrum UV filter with peak absorption at 385nm (long UVA-1); lipophilic; highly photostable; organic filter that also absorbs UVB; prevents deep UVA damage that penetrates farthest into skin
regulatory_notes: Targets previously unprotected long UVA-1 wavelengths

name: Tinted Iron Oxides
mechanism: Mineral pigments providing visible light and blue light protection beyond UV spectrum; particularly important for melanin-rich skin and melasma; absorbs and scatters high-energy visible (HEV) light

interactions.note: Tinted zinc oxide provides UV + visible light protection
Tinted TiO2 extends protection into visible spectrum

regulatory_notes: Not standalone sun protection but important for melasma
Often added to mineral sunscreens for tint and extra protection

name: Ceramide NS
mechanism: N-acylated sphingosine ceramide; key structural component of lamellar lipid bilayers in stratum corneum; maintains skin barrier integrity; works synergistically with ceramide NP and EOP in 3:1:1:1 ratios

interactions.note: Optimal ceramide blend with NP and EOP
Essential trio: ceramides + cholesterol + fatty acids

regulatory_notes: Often combined with NP and EOP in formulations

name: Fatty Acids
mechanism: Essential barrier lipid component (~25% of stratum corneum lipids); includes palmitic, stearic, oleic, linoleic acids; maintains lamellar structure with ceramides and cholesterol; linoleic acid is specifically essential for barrier function
concerns_exacerbated: acne (at very high oleic acid concentrations)
contraindications: High oleic acid can disrupt barrier in acne-prone skin
regulatory_notes: Linoleic acid is essential (cannot be synthesized)
Balanced fatty acid profile matters more than individual fatty acids

name: Hexapeptide-8
mechanism: SNARE complex inhibitor; prevents formation of SNARE complex required for neurotransmitter release; reduces muscle contraction intensity; topical alternative to injectable neuromodulators

interactions.note: Redundant mechanism; use one or the other

name: Malic Acid
mechanism: AHA found naturally in apples and other fruits; larger than glycolic but smaller than mandelic; disrupts corneocyte adhesion; chelates metals; stimulates exfoliation; improves skin hydration and elasticity
regulatory_notes: Commonly used in professional peels at 10-20%
Often combined with citric and tartaric in formulations

name: Tartaric Acid
mechanism: AHA from grapes and tamarinds; keratolytic exfoliant; chelates calcium ions disrupting corneocyte cohesion; antioxidant properties; improves skin texture and radiance

interactions.note: Redundant; often combined in multi-acid blends

regulatory_notes: Less commonly used as primary AHA
Often added to support glycolic or lactic acid blends

name: Camellia Oil
mechanism: Rich in oleic acid (80%+), vitamins A, D, E, and polyphenols; molecular structure similar to human sebum; penetrates deeply; anti-inflammatory; promotes collagen synthesis; antioxidant
regulatory_notes: Camellia sinensis (tea) vs Camellia japonica (ornamental) both used

name: Ceramide Complex
mechanism: Pre-formulated blend of ceramides, cholesterol, and fatty acids in optimal physiological ratios (typically 3:1:1:1 or similar); mimics natural skin lipid composition; restores lamellar barrier structure rapidly
regulatory_notes: Eucerin and CeraVe use patented ceramide complexes
Multiple ceramide types (NP, AP, EOP) plus cholesterol and fatty acids

name: Melatonin
mechanism: Hormone with potent antioxidant properties in skin; activates melatonin receptors in keratinocytes and fibroblasts; protects against UV-induced mitochondrial DNA damage; stimulates proliferation of keratinocytes
regulatory_notes: Topical application does not affect sleep cycles

name: Hexapeptide-9
mechanism: Signal peptide mimicking natural skin processes; stimulates collagen III synthesis (youth collagen); promotes keratinocyte proliferation; improves skin radiance and smoothness
regulatory_notes: Emerging peptide with promising early data

name: Palmitoyl Tetrapeptide-7 (Enhanced)
mechanism: Anti-inflammatory signal peptide; reduces IL-6 production in response to UV damage; suppresses overactive immune response in skin; complements collagen-stimulating peptides for comprehensive anti-aging

interactions.note: Matrixyl 3000: combined collagen + anti-inflammatory signal peptides

name: Sodium Ascorbyl Phosphate
mechanism: Stable, water-soluble vitamin C derivative; converts to ascorbic acid via enzymatic cleavage in skin; antioxidant, collagen-stimulating, and tyrosinase-inhibiting properties; antimicrobial against C. acnes

interactions.note: Myth of incompatibility debunked; safe and effective together

regulatory_notes: One of most stable vitamin C derivatives
Does not require low pH formulation

name: Glutathione
mechanism: Tripeptide antioxidant (glutamyl-cysteinyl-glycine); master intracellular antioxidant neutralizing free radicals; recycles vitamins C and E; inhibits tyrosinase and switches melanin production from eumelanin to phaeomelanin; detoxifies
regulatory_notes: Poor topical penetration; liposomal/encapsulated forms preferred
Very unstable; oxidizes easily in formulation
Popular in oral supplements for skin brightening

name: Madecassoside
mechanism: Most active pentacyclic triterpenoid from Centella asiatica; stimulates collagen I/III synthesis and fibroblast proliferation; anti-inflammatory via NF-kB and MAPK inhibition; promotes wound healing, angiogenesis, and capillary strengthening
regulatory_notes: Pure form of Centella's most active compound
More potent than whole Centella extract
Popular in K-beauty and French pharmacy

name: Bakuchiol
mechanism: Meroterpene from Psoralea corylifolia seeds; activates retinoid receptors and retinol-like gene expression without retinoid side effects; stimulates collagen I/III/IV, reduces MMPs, inhibits melanin via tyrosinase; anti-inflammatory and antioxidant

interactions.note: Redundant; choose one retinoid or retinoid-alternative

regulatory_notes: Not structurally a retinoid but functionally similar

name: Tranexamic Acid
mechanism: Synthetic amino acid inhibiting plasminogen activator; reduces arachidonic acid and prostaglandin release; decreases UV-induced melanocyte activity and melanin synthesis; reduces vascular component of melasma via anti-angiogenic effects
contraindications: Pregnancy (oral form contraindicated; topical use with caution)
History of thrombosis or clotting disorders
Oral: do not combine with hormonal contraceptives without medical supervision

name: Centella Asiatica
mechanism: Rich in triterpenoids (asiaticoside, madecassoside, asiatic acid, madecassic acid); stimulates collagen I/III synthesis; anti-inflammatory via NF-kB inhibition; promotes wound healing and angiogenesis; strengthens capillaries
regulatory_notes: Traditional medicinal herb with modern clinical validation
Madecassoside is the most active compound
Wild harvesting concerns; sustainable sourcing preferred

name: Alpha Arbutin
mechanism: Glycosylated hydroquinone that slowly releases hydroquinone via hydrolysis; inhibits tyrosinase without cytotoxicity to melanocytes; more stable and less irritating than beta-arbutin; safe for long-term use

name: Coenzyme Q10
mechanism: Lipid-soluble antioxidant in mitochondrial electron transport chain; neutralizes free radicals in cell membranes; energizes skin cells; anti-inflammatory; supports collagen; levels decline significantly after age 30

interactions.note: CoQ10 is regenerated by vitamin E

regulatory_notes: Naturally produced in body; declines after age 30
Ubiquinol (reduced form) more bioactive than ubiquinone

name: Zinc PCA
mechanism: Zinc complexed with pyrrolidone carboxylic acid (natural moisturizing factor component); sebum-regulating; antimicrobial against C. acnes; anti-inflammatory; maintains skin hydration while controlling oil production

interactions.note: Combined oil control and pore minimization

regulatory_notes: More hydrating than zinc oxide or zinc sulfate

name: Sulfur
mechanism: Keratolytic and antibacterial; converts to pentathionic acid and hydrogen sulfide inhibiting C. acnes; antifungal against Malassezia; reduces sebum production; comedolytic; anti-inflammatory
regulatory_notes: One of oldest acne treatments (used since Roman times)
OTC acne treatment in US at 3-10%

name: Jojoba Oil
mechanism: Liquid wax ester structurally similar to human sebum (25% wax esters); non-comedogenic; regulates sebum production via signaling; anti-inflammatory (myristic acid); antimicrobial; forms protective film without heavy occlusion
regulatory_notes: Actually a wax, not an oil

name: Epidermal Growth Factor (EGF)
mechanism: Recombinant 53-amino acid polypeptide mimicking human EGF; binds EGFR activating MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways; stimulates keratinocyte proliferation, fibroblast activity, and collagen synthesis; accelerates wound healing
regulatory_notes: Controversial efficacy in cosmetic topical use
Nobel Prize 1986 for EGF discovery (Cohen & Levi-Montalcini)

name: Malic Acid
mechanism: AHA found naturally in apples and fruits; larger than glycolic but smaller than mandelic; disrupts corneocyte adhesion via calcium chelation; stimulates exfoliation; improves skin hydration and elasticity; milder than glycolic acid
regulatory_notes: Commonly used in professional peels at 10-20%
Often combined with citric and tartaric in multi-fruit acid blends

name: Tartaric Acid
mechanism: AHA from grapes, tamarinds, and wine; keratolytic exfoliant; chelates calcium ions disrupting corneocyte cohesion; antioxidant properties; improves skin texture and radiance; supports other AHAs in formulations

interactions.note: Redundant; often combined in multi-acid blends

regulatory_notes: Less commonly used as primary AHA
Often added to support glycolic or lactic acid blends

name: Betaine Salicylate
mechanism: BHA derivative combining salicylic acid with betaine (trimethylglycine); gentler alternative to salicylic acid; oil-soluble exfoliant penetrating pores; anti-inflammatory; betaine component provides moisturizing benefits reducing dryness
regulatory_notes: COSRX BHA Blackhead Power Liquid uses 4% betaine salicylate
Gentler alternative for sensitive skin types

name: Sodium Hyaluronate
mechanism: Salt form of hyaluronic acid with smaller molecular size enabling deeper penetration; holds 1000x its weight in water; stimulates CD44 receptor signaling; different molecular weights target different skin depths; supports wound healing and barrier function

interactions.note: Sodium hyaluronate is the more bioavailable salt form

regulatory_notes: Low molecular weight penetrates deeper; high MW stays surface
Most common form in skincare formulations
Sodium salt is more stable than free hyaluronic acid

name: Panthenol
mechanism: Converted to pantothenic acid (vitamin B5) in skin; penetrates deeply; attracts and binds water; stimulates fibroblast proliferation; promotes wound healing; reduces TEWL; anti-inflammatory; supports barrier repair

interactions.note: B-vitamin synergy for barrier and hydration

regulatory_notes: FDA-approved for wound healing at higher concentrations
Extremely well-tolerated by all skin types

name: Adenosine
mechanism: Nucleoside involved in cellular energy transfer (ATP/ADP); increases collagen I/III synthesis via fibroblast activation; inhibits elastase (MMP-12) preventing elastin breakdown; anti-inflammatory via adenosine receptor activation; energizes skin cells
regulatory_notes: Often combined with niacinamide in K-beauty

name: Hexapeptide-9
mechanism: Signal peptide mimicking natural skin regeneration processes; specifically stimulates collagen III synthesis (youth collagen prevalent in young skin); promotes keratinocyte proliferation; improves skin radiance, smoothness, and firmness
regulatory_notes: Emerging peptide with promising early data

name: Coenzyme Q10 (Ubiquinone)
mechanism: Endogenous lipid-soluble antioxidant in mitochondrial electron transport chain; protects mitochondria from oxidative damage; energizes skin cells; supports collagen; levels decline significantly with age; anti-inflammatory
regulatory_notes: Naturally produced in body; declines after age 30
Ubiquinol (reduced form) more bioactive than ubiquinone

name: Melatonin
mechanism: Hormone with potent direct and indirect antioxidant properties in skin; activates melatonin receptors in keratinocytes and fibroblasts; protects against UV-induced mitochondrial DNA damage; stimulates keratinocyte proliferation; anti-inflammatory
regulatory_notes: Topical application does not affect sleep cycles
Used by brands like ISDIN in sun care
More potent antioxidant than vitamins C and E in some studies

name: Ceramide Complex
mechanism: Pre-formulated blend of ceramides (NP, AP, EOP), cholesterol, and fatty acids in optimal physiological ratios (typically 3:1:1:1 or similar); mimics natural skin lipid composition; restores lamellar barrier structure rapidly and effectively
regulatory_notes: Eucerin and CeraVe use patented ceramide complexes
Multiple ceramide types (NP, AP, EOP) plus cholesterol and fatty acids

name: Avobenzone
mechanism: Organic UVA filter absorbing UVA-I (340-400nm); most widely used UVA chemical filter in US sunscreens; degrades in sunlight without photostabilizers (octocrylene, Mexoryl); requires stabilization for effective protection

interactions.note: Zinc oxide can degrade avobenzone; formulation compatibility matters

contraindications: Sensitive skin may react to chemical filters
regulatory_notes: Max 3% in US (up to 5% in some formulations)
Most common UVA filter in US sunscreens
Unstable without photostabilizers - key formulation challenge

name: Glycyrrhiza Glabra Root Extract
mechanism: Inhibits tyrosinase and melanin production; anti-inflammatory via glabridin
regulatory_notes: Glabridin is the primary active compound

name: Chamomilla Recutita Flower Extract
mechanism: Anti-inflammatory flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin); bisabolol precursor; wound healing

name: Sclerocarya Birrea Seed Oil
mechanism: Rich in oleic acid, antioxidants (Vitamin C, E), and amino acids; lightweight moisturizer
regulatory_notes: High oleic acid content — comedogenic for some
